title: Theory of Supercritical Coupling And Generalized Bound States in the Continuum
authors: Sergio Balestrieri, Bruno Miranda, Silvia Romano, Gianluigi Zito
abstract: Bound states in the continuum (BICs) arise from destructive interference suppressing radiation despite spectral overlap with the continuum. Here we show that Friedrich--Wintgen interference naturally emerges from a bright--dark supermode decomposition of resonances coupled through a shared radiation channel. In this basis, any finite leakage of a quasi-BIC induces a causality-driven reactive coupling enabling non-Hermitian pumping of the dark sector. We derive the optimal condition for this process and show that it corresponds to the supercritical coupling regime previously identified in [Nature 626, 765 (2024)], while naturally recovering universal quasi-BIC asymmetry scaling. Extending the theory to Dirac-like dispersions in photonic crystal slabs, we identify an open-Dirac singularity where the Dirac gap matches the supercritical regime. A four-wave Hamiltonian quantitatively reproduces rigorous coupled-wave analysis, revealing the breakdown of conventional critical coupling. Near this regime, absorptive cross-coupling induces coherent absorption interference and enables suppression of effective dissipative losses beyond conventional material limits. These results motivate the concept of a generalized bound state in the continuum (gBIC) as a limiting non-Hermitian state where radiative and effective gain compensate, producing a true divergence of the total quality factor. Overall, this work establishes a unified framework connecting BIC interference, Dirac topology, and non-Hermitian physics for ultra-high-Q enhancement and loss engineering in open photonic systems.
summary: This paper presents a unified theory connecting BICs, Dirac topology, and non-Hermitian physics in photonic systems. It identifies a supercritical coupling regime that optimizes energy transfer to dark modes and proposes a generalized BIC where radiative and absorptive losses are perfectly compensated. This work offers a new way to engineer ultra-high-Q resonances and control dissipation in open photonic structures.
main_problem: Developing a unified theory to connect Bound States in the Continuum (BICs), Dirac topology, and non-Hermitian physics, specifically understanding how supercritical coupling and reactive coupling affect energy transfer and mode enhancement.
main_result: The authors establish a framework where supercritical coupling enables optimal dark-mode pumping and propose a 'generalized BIC' (gBIC) where radiative and effective gain/loss compensate to produce a divergence in the total quality factor.
method: The study uses a non-Hermitian Hamiltonian framework, supermode decomposition, and Kramers-Kronig causality relations, validated by Rigorous Coupled-Wave Analysis (RCWA) and four-wave Hamiltonian models.
model_or_system: Open photonic systems, specifically photonic crystal slabs (PhCS) and metasurfaces, modeled using a non-Hermitian Hamiltonian describing coupled bright and dark resonances interacting through a shared radiation channel.
key_observables: Total quality factor (Q), radiative decay rate, effective absorption, dark-mode enhancement factor, Petermann factor, and absorptance.
important_parameters: Reactive coupling (kappa'), supercritical coupling regime (kappa_sc), Friedrich-Wintgen condition, Dirac gap, and the open-Dirac singularity.
main_assumptions: Initial models assume a single shared radiation channel (rank-1 radiation matrix) and a diagonal absorption operator.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows dynamic evolution of mode populations; Figure 6 shows eigenmode dispersion, decay rates, and the Petermann factor in a four-wave system.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of BIC-non-Hermitian connections, develops the supermode decomposition and reactive coupling theory, extends the model to Dirac-like dispersions and three/four-mode systems, and concludes with the proposal of gBICs and loss engineering applications.
why_interesting: It provides deep insights into non-Hermitian dynamics, specifically how non-orthogonality and exceptional points can be leveraged for loss engineering and mode enhancement, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Dynamic evolution of dark and bright mode populations under continuous wave drive at frequency ν = ω/2π = 299 THz (λ = 1µm), for different values of κ′ around κsc, and for fixed parameters of radiative and non radiative losses as displayed.

FIG. 2. Dynamic evolution of dark and bright mode populations under continuous wave drive at frequency ω, for different values of κ′ around κsc, and for fixed parameters of radiative and non radiative losses as displayed.

FIG. 3. Dynamics of dark and bright mode populations un- der pulsed wave excitation at frequency ν = ω/2π = 299 THz (λ = 1 µm), at κ′ = κsc, for fixed decay parameters as dis- played. The population of the dark mode saturates at a level imposed by γabs, which is reached with a characteristic time ∼1/γabs. The pulse maximum is normalized to the bright mode peak at t = 0, for visualization. The bright mode max- imum decays with time stabilizing at its steady state value after a sufficient number of pulses. As the train of pulses (500) stops, the dark mode decays obeying γabs = 10−6ωd (nanosecond time scale) since γd ≪γabs.

FIG. 4. Quality factor of the dark mode as a function of the in plane momentum and ratio γ12/√γ1γ2 of the original degenerate modes (ω1 = ω2): only when the single radiation channel condition is fulfilled (ratio 1), the dark mode has diverging radiative Q-factor because of interference.

FIG. 5. (a) Real part of the eigenmodes (1 and 2, respectively) after diagonalization of the Hamiltonian representing degenerate Dirac modes at Γ-point, γ1 = γ2 = 5 meV, with vg = 127meVµm, as a function of coupling, from top to bottom respectively κ = 10, 1.0, 0.1 meV. The linewidth of the eigenmodes is given by the size of the data points. (b) Associated decay rates. (c) Associated Petermann factor, diverging at EPs, revealing nearly coalescent vectors for reducing gap 2κ. (d) Conventional inner product of right eigenvectors (biorthogonality is verfied, not shown), revealing mutual non-orthogonality. The dark mode EP has radiative loss converging to zero at Γ.
